kcal.low: Your total calorie intake is below the recommended amount. Add another balanced meal.

carb.low: Your carbohydrate intake is low. Supplement with brown rice or whole grains.
carb.high: You ate a bit too much carbohydrate. Try cutting back on refined carbs.

protein.low: Your protein intake is low. Supplement with chicken breast, eggs, or tofu.
protein.high: Your protein intake has exceeded the recommended amount. Adjust it to avoid straining your kidneys.

fat.low: Your fat intake is low. Add healthy fats like nuts or olive oil.
fat.high: You ate a bit too much fat. Try cutting back on fried and greasy foods.

sugar.high: You ate more sugar than recommended. Cut back on sweet drinks and desserts.

na.high: Your sodium intake is high. Cut back on broths, processed foods, and salty dishes.

chol.high: Your cholesterol intake is high. Adjust greasy meats and organ meats.

advice.balanced: The ratio of nutrients you've consumed so far is very close to the recommended guidelines. Keep up the balanced eating for the rest of the day.

riskFactors.0: Abdominal obesity and insulin resistance (key risk factors for type 2 diabetes in Koreans)
riskFactors.1: Lack of physical activity and a diet centered on refined carbohydrates
riskFactors.2: Family history, age 40 or older, accompanied by hypertension and dyslipidemia

diabetesReportPage.title: Diabetes Mellitus
diabetesReportPage.intro: Diabetes is a metabolic disease in which blood sugar is chronically elevated due to insulin secretion deficiency or insulin resistance (WHO). Per the Korean Diabetes Association 2023 clinical guidelines, it is diagnosed based on fasting blood sugar, HbA1c, and postprandial blood sugar; systematic blood sugar management and lifestyle correction are key to preventing complications.

overview.heading: What is diabetes?
overview.paragraph1: Diabetes is a group of chronic metabolic diseases that share hyperglycemia as a common feature (WHO). Chronic blood sugar elevation causes microvascular complications in the retina, kidneys, and nerves, as well as macrovascular complications such as myocardial infarction and stroke. According to the KDCA Korea National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (2022), about 14% of Korean adults aged 30 or older have diabetes, and nearly half do when prediabetes is included.
overview.paragraph2: Normally, blood sugar is regulated by insulin secreted from pancreatic beta cells. In diabetes, this regulatory mechanism breaks down due to insufficient insulin secretion or insulin resistance, and if left untreated, it leads to serious complications such as cardiovascular disease, kidney failure, and blindness.

fasting.title: Fasting blood sugar 126 mg/dL or higher
fasting.subTitle: Korean Diabetes Association 2023 criteria
fasting.desc: Blood sugar of 126 mg/dL or higher after fasting for 8 hours or more indicates diabetes; 100-125 mg/dL indicates impaired fasting glucose (prediabetes). A 2-hour postprandial blood sugar of 200 mg/dL or higher is also a diagnostic criterion.

hba1c.title: Glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) 6.5% or higher
hba1c.subTitle: Long-term blood sugar indicator
hba1c.desc: Reflects the average blood sugar over the past 2-3 months. HbA1c of 6.5% or higher indicates diabetes, and 5.7-6.4% is classified as prediabetes (WHO and Korean Diabetes Association).

ogtt.title: 75 g oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT)
ogtt.subTitle: Confirmation and complication assessment
ogtt.desc: Diabetes is diagnosed if blood sugar is 200 mg/dL or higher 2 hours after ingesting 75 g of glucose. After diagnosis, target-organ complications are assessed through fundus, microalbuminuria, and nerve examinations.

type1.title: Type 1 Diabetes
type1.desc: A form in which insulin secretion is absolutely deficient due to autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells. It mainly develops in childhood and adolescence, and survival is difficult without external insulin administration.

type2.title: Type 2 Diabetes
type2.desc: The most common form, accounting for more than 90% of Korean diabetes patients, combining insulin resistance with relative insulin secretion deficiency. It is strongly associated with obesity, lack of exercise, and aging.

avoid.0: Simple sugars and refined carbohydrates (sugar, soda, white rice, rice cakes)
avoid.1: Sugar-sweetened beverages and excessive fruit juice
avoid.2: Processed foods containing saturated and trans fats

recommend.0: Whole grains rich in dietary fiber (brown rice, multigrain, oats)
recommend.1: Fresh vegetables and quality protein (beans, tofu, fish)
recommend.2: Unsaturated fatty acids (nuts, oily fish, olive oil)

activity.desc: Per WHO recommendations, 150-300 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic exercise per week and strength training at least twice a week are recommended. In particular, walking 30 minutes to 1 hour after meals effectively suppresses postprandial blood sugar spikes.

step1.title: The "three polys" symptoms - early signs
step1.desc: Polydipsia, polyuria, and polyphagia are typical. This is because high blood sugar causes sugar to spill into the urine, leading to osmotic diuresis.

step2.title: Warning symptoms - weight loss and vision changes
step2.desc: Reduced insulin action breaks down fat and protein instead of glucose, causing rapid weight loss over a short period. Blurred vision, frequent infections, and tingling in the hands and feet are also warning signs.

step3.title: Chronic complications
step3.desc: If left untreated for a long time, systemic complications such as diabetic retinopathy, diabetic nephropathy (the leading cause of dialysis), diabetic neuropathy, myocardial infarction, and stroke can occur.

symptoms.caution: Early diabetes is often asymptomatic, so the KDCA recommends annual fasting blood sugar and HbA1c tests for those aged 40 or older or with risk factors.

kdca.source: Korea Disease Control and Prevention Agency (KDCA)
kdca.detail: Korea National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey 2022, chronic disease statistics

riskFactors.0: About 40% of Korean adults aged 30+ have dyslipidemia (KDCA 2022)
riskFactors.1: Progresses to atherosclerosis and cardiovascular/cerebrovascular disease without noticeable symptoms
riskFactors.2: Diet, exercise, weight management, and drug therapy such as statins are essential together

dyslipidemiaReportPage.title: Hyperlipidemia
dyslipidemiaReportPage.subtitle: A chronic metabolic disease diagnosed per the Korean Society of Lipid and Atherosclerosis (KSoLA) 2022 guidelines when LDL is 160, total cholesterol 240, or triglycerides 200 mg/dL or higher, or HDL is below 40 mg/dL.

overview.heading: What is hyperlipidemia?
overview.paragraph1: Dyslipidemia is defined as elevated blood LDL cholesterol, total cholesterol, or triglycerides, or low HDL cholesterol (KSoLA). The KDCA 2022 survey reported that about 40% of Korean adults aged 30+ have dyslipidemia, and prevalence rises sharply with age.
overview.paragraph2: Excess lipids form plaques in the vascular endothelium, accelerating atherosclerosis and ultimately causing angina, myocardial infarction, stroke, and peripheral vascular disease. As it often accompanies metabolic syndrome, diabetes, and hypertension, integrated risk assessment and management of LDL target levels are key.

card1.title: LDL 160 / TC 240 / TG 200 mg/dL
card1.subTitle: KSoLA 2022 diagnostic criteria
card1.desc: Dyslipidemia is diagnosed when any one of the following applies: LDL 160 mg/dL or higher, total cholesterol 240 mg/dL or higher, triglycerides 200 mg/dL or higher, or HDL below 40 mg/dL.

card2.title: 9-12 hour fasting blood test
card2.subTitle: Lipid panel measurement
card2.desc: For triglyceride accuracy, blood is drawn after 9-12 hours of fasting to measure TC, LDL, HDL, and TG. Retesting at 1-2 week intervals rules out temporary fluctuations.

card3.title: Cardiovascular risk assessment
card3.subTitle: Analysis of accompanying risk factors
card3.desc: Atherosclerosis is checked with carotid ultrasound and coronary CT, and the 10-year cardiovascular risk is assessed by combining age, hypertension, diabetes, smoking, and family history to set the LDL target.

lipidLowering.title: Lowering lipid levels
lipidLowering.item: Statins are the first-line drug, inhibiting hepatic cholesterol synthesis to reduce LDL by 30-50%. Ezetimibe and PCSK9 inhibitors are added when needed.

monitoring.title: Continuous monitoring
monitoring.item: Maintain target LDL by risk level (below 70 mg/dL for high-risk, below 100 mg/dL for moderate, below 130 mg/dL for low-risk) and monitor with regular blood tests.

avoid.heading: Foods to avoid
avoid.item1: Saturated and trans fats (pork belly, butter, fried food, margarine)
avoid.item2: Excessive drinking (a leading cause of triglyceride spikes)
avoid.item3: Simple sugars and high-fructose corn syrup (soda, instant coffee, desserts)

recommend.heading: Recommendations
recommend.item1: Whole grains, oats, and beans rich in soluble fiber

physicalActivity.paragraph: Per WHO recommendations, 150-300 minutes of moderate-intensity or 75-150 minutes of high-intensity aerobic exercise per week raises HDL and lowers TG. Losing just 5-10% of body weight significantly improves LDL and TG.

step1.title: Early Silent Phase
step1.desc: Dyslipidemia itself causes almost no pain or visible changes and is found only through regular blood tests (national screening every 4 years for those aged 40+).

step2.title: Warning Signs
step2.desc: In severe cases, eyelid xanthelasma, corneal arcus, and Achilles tendon xanthomas may appear, and chest tightness resembling angina may accompany them.

step3.title: Chronic Complications
step3.desc: Progressing atherosclerosis causes myocardial infarction, cerebral infarction, and peripheral vascular disease, and when triglycerides exceed 500 mg/dL, the risk of acute pancreatitis rises sharply.

symptoms.caution: Manage comorbidities such as hypertension, diabetes, and obesity together, and lipid test monitoring is needed every 6 months to 1 year depending on risk level.

ksola.source: Korean Society of Lipid and Atherosclerosis (KSoLA)

kdca.source: Korea Disease Control and Prevention Agency (KDCA)
kdca.detail: National Health and Nutrition Survey 2022, Chronic Disease Status Report

riskFactors.0: Incidence in men is about 3-4 times higher than in women, surging in the 40s-50s
riskFactors.1: Over half of acute attacks begin in the first metatarsophalangeal joint of the big toe
riskFactors.2: Diet control, weight management, and urate-lowering therapy are essential together

goutReportPage.title: Gout
goutReportPage.intro: A chronic inflammatory arthritis caused by hyperuricemia, defined as serum uric acid above 7.0 mg/dL, in which monosodium urate (MSU) crystals deposit in the joints (ACR/Korean College of Rheumatology 2020).

overview.heading: What is gout?
overview.paragraph1:
Gout occurs when abnormal purine metabolism raises serum uric acid above 7.0 mg/dL (hyperuricemia), causing monosodium urate (MSU) crystals to deposit in joints and soft tissues.
                Crystals deposited in the joints trigger a rapid inflammatory response, causing acute attacks accompanied by severe pain, swelling, redness, and warmth. The number of gout patients in Korea has roughly doubled over the past 10 years.
overview.paragraph2: Gout goes beyond a simple joint disease and is closely linked to metabolic syndrome, hypertension, and chronic kidney disease. If left unmanaged, it can progress to chronic tophaceous gout, kidney stones, and kidney failure.

card1.title: Serum uric acid > 7.0 mg/dL
card1.subTitle: Hyperuricemia criterion
card1.desc: Serum uric acid above 7.0 mg/dL is defined as hyperuricemia (ACR/KCR 2020). However, levels may appear normal during an acute attack, so retesting at a different time is needed.

card2.title: Confirming MSU crystals in joint fluid
card2.subTitle: Gold standard for diagnosis
card2.desc: Diagnosis is confirmed by identifying needle-shaped monosodium urate (MSU) crystals showing negative birefringence under polarized microscopy in synovial fluid obtained by joint aspiration.

card3.title: Ultrasound / DECT imaging diagnosis
card3.subTitle: Non-invasive diagnosis
card3.desc: Gout can be diagnosed non-invasively by identifying the 'Double Contour' sign on joint ultrasound or by visualizing urate crystal deposits with dual-energy CT (DECT).

acute.heading: Acute-phase relief
acute.item1: NSAIDs, colchicine (low dose), and steroids are used alone or in combination to suppress inflammation within 24 hours of an attack.

longTerm.heading: Long-term prevention (ULT)
longTerm.item1: Take urate-lowering drugs (allopurinol, febuxostat) consistently to keep serum uric acid below 6.0 mg/dL (below 5.0 mg/dL for tophaceous gout).

avoid.heading: Foods to avoid
avoid.item1: Alcohol such as beer and spirits (promotes uric acid production)
avoid.item3: High-purine fish such as animal organs, anchovies, sardines, and mackerel

recommend.heading: Recommendations
recommend.item1: Dairy products such as low-fat milk and yogurt (help excrete uric acid)
recommend.item2: Vegetables, cherries, and vitamin C-rich foods
recommend.item3: Complex carbohydrates based on whole grains

hydration.description: Drinking at least 2L of water daily promotes uric acid excretion through the kidneys and prevents urate stone formation. Sweetened and fructose drinks actually increase uric acid production and should be avoided.

step1.title: Acute gouty arthritis
step1.desc: It usually begins abruptly at night, with the joint turning red and swollen and severe pain developing. Over 50% of first attacks occur in the first metatarsophalangeal joint of the big toe.

step2.title: Intercritical gout
step2.desc: An asymptomatic period after an attack subsides. It is easy to stop treatment, but without uric acid control the interval between recurrences gradually shortens.

step3.title: Chronic tophaceous gout
step3.desc: If left untreated for years, urate crystals form tophi that cause joint destruction and deformity, and lead to declining kidney function through kidney stones and urate nephropathy.

symptoms.caution: Managing comorbid metabolic syndrome such as hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, and obesity is crucial to gout prognosis, and regular uric acid and kidney function tests are needed.

1.detail: Guideline for the Management of Gout, 2020

2.source: Korea Disease Control and Prevention Agency (KDCA)

3.detail: Recommendations for the Management of Gout, 2016 (updated)

header.title: Health Report
header.subtitle: Check your recent health metrics at a glance.

locked.title: This is an Individual/Family plan exclusive feature
locked.description: Health reports are available on the Individual or Family plan.

riskFactors.0: About 28% of Korean adults aged 30+ have hypertension (KDCA 2022)
riskFactors.1: Progresses without symptoms; target-organ damage may already exist at diagnosis
riskFactors.2: A low-salt diet, weight management, and medication must go together

hypertensionReportPage.title: Hypertension
hypertensionReportPage.intro:
A chronic condition diagnosed under the Korean Society of Hypertension 2022 guidelines when office blood pressure is 140/90 mmHg or higher, or home blood pressure is 135/85 mmHg or higher.
            Known as the "silent killer" because it has no early symptoms, systematic management is essential to prevent complications such as stroke and myocardial infarction.

overview.heading: What is hypertension?
overview.paragraph1:
Hypertension is a state in which the pressure on the blood vessel walls continuously exceeds the normal range (WHO). According to the KDCA's Korea National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey 2022,
                about 28% of Korean adults aged 30+ have hypertension, exceeding half among those aged 65+. Chronic pressure elevation damages the vascular endothelium and accelerates atherosclerosis, leading to fatal complications such as stroke and myocardial infarction.
overview.paragraph2: Hypertension is closely linked to metabolic syndrome, and if left unmanaged it raises the risk of heart failure, kidney failure (dialysis), retinopathy, and dementia. Continuous management through home blood pressure monitoring and regular medical visits is key.

card1.title: Office blood pressure 140/90 mmHg or higher
card1.subTitle: Korean Society of Hypertension 2022 criteria
card1.desc: Hypertension is diagnosed when office blood pressure measured on two or more visits is 140 systolic or 90 mmHg diastolic or higher. A reading of 130-139/80-89 is the caution stage.

card2.title: Home blood pressure 135/85 mmHg or higher
card2.subTitle: Home / 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure measurement
card2.desc: Home or 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure is measured to distinguish white-coat and masked hypertension. The diagnostic criteria are an ambulatory daytime average of 135/85 or higher and a nighttime average of 120/70 or higher.

card3.title: Target-organ damage assessment
card3.subTitle: Complication screening tests
card3.desc: Target-organ damage such as left ventricular hypertrophy, retinopathy, and kidney damage is assessed through ECG, echocardiography, fundus exam, urine test (microalbuminuria), and kidney function tests.

initial.heading: Initial blood pressure control
initial.item1: ACE inhibitors, ARBs (angiotensin receptor blockers), calcium channel blockers (CCBs), and diuretics are used as first-line agents, given alone or in combination depending on the patient's condition to safely reach the target blood pressure.

longTerm.heading: Long-term maintenance
longTerm.item1: Through home blood pressure monitoring, regular exercise, and consistent adherence to the prescription, the goal is to maintain below 140/90 mmHg for the general adult, and below 130/80 mmHg when diabetes or kidney disease is present.

avoid.heading: Foods to limit
avoid.item1: High-sodium foods (ramyeon, salted seafood, kimchi, processed meats)
avoid.item2: Excessive alcohol (limit to 2 drinks/day for men, 1 for women or less)
avoid.item3: Saturated fat, trans fat, and simple sugars

recommend.heading: Recommendations
recommend.item3: Whole grains and lean protein (fish, beans, chicken breast)

lifestyle.description: The WHO recommends 150-300 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic exercise per week and advises limiting salt intake to under 5 g per day (2,000 mg sodium). Losing 1 kg of body weight lowers blood pressure by about 1 mmHg.

step1.title: Early asymptomatic stage (Silent Phase)
step1.desc: Most patients do not notice their elevated blood pressure. This is why it is called the "silent killer," and it is detected only through home blood pressure monitoring and regular health checkups.

step2.title: Warning Signs
step2.desc: When blood pressure rises sharply, occipital headache, dizziness, nosebleeds, and blurred vision may appear; a hypertensive emergency of 180/120 mmHg or higher requires immediate emergency room care.

step3.title: Chronic Complications
step3.desc: If left untreated long-term, atherosclerosis progresses and leads to target-organ damage such as stroke, myocardial infarction, heart failure, chronic kidney disease, and hypertensive retinopathy.

symptoms.caution: It is important to manage coexisting conditions such as diabetes, dyslipidemia, and obesity together, and to detect complications early through regular checkups every 6 months to 1 year.

2.source: Korea Disease Control and Prevention Agency (KDCA)

3.detail: Guidelines for the Management of Arterial Hypertension, 2023

dailyRecord.title: Daily Health Records
dailyRecord.description: Log and manage all your daily health records on one screen, from diet, exercise, water intake, weight, blood pressure, and blood sugar to medication.

stats.title: Record Statistics & Reports
stats.description: See the flow of your changes at a glance with period-based statistics and visualized graphs, and receive a weekly comprehensive report.

goal.title: Custom Goal Setting
goal.description: Set goals for weight, water intake, and calories consumed/burned, and get reminders aligned with your daily routine (wake-up, meals, bedtime).

medication.title: Medication Management
medication.description: A single photo of your prescription automatically registers the medications you take, and time-based medication reminders help you never miss a dose.

diseaseGuide.title: Disease Guides
diseaseGuide.description: Get reliable medical information and management methods for major chronic conditions such as hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, obesity, gout, and osteoporosis.

community.title: Health Community
community.description: Share experiences with people who have the same concerns, and freely exchange questions, know-how, and daily life on category boards.

record.title: Record
record.description: Just casually log today's meals, exercise, blood pressure, blood sugar, and weight, and your data steadily builds up.

analyze.title: Analyze
analyze.description: AI analyzes your accumulated data to show you trends, warning signs, and areas that need improvement.

manage.title: Manage
manage.description: We help you sustain healthy habits through custom goals, routines, medication reminders, and community encouragement.

hero.description: Ballife brings together your scattered health data, from diet, exercise, blood pressure, and blood sugar to medication, in one place, helping you sustain your health with AI-analyzed insights and community encouragement.

featuresSection.title: Everything You Need for Health Management, in One Place
featuresSection.subtitle: Make health management easier and smarter with the 6 core features Ballife offers.

aiSection.description: From health questions and meal photo analysis to medication reminders. We provide personalized advice tailored to your lifestyle.

points.interaction: Check interactions with your current medications
points.interpretation: Interpretation of blood pressure & sugar readings

aiSection.chatUser: My blood sugar was 180 after lunch today. Is that okay?
aiSection.chatAi: A 2-hour post-meal blood sugar of 180 mg/dL is somewhat higher than the normal threshold (under 140). Meals centered on fiber-rich grains and vegetables, plus a light 20-minute walk after eating, can help.

cta.title: The First Step Toward a Better You Tomorrow
cta.subtitle: Join Ballife now and start a healthier lifestyle with better users.

riskFactors.0: About 38% of Korean adults are obese (BMI ≥ 25, KDCA 2022)
riskFactors.1: Inflammatory cytokine secretion from fat cells causes metabolic and hormonal imbalance
riskFactors.2: Combine diet, exercise, and behavioral therapy; consider medication or surgery if needed

intro.line1: Per WHO Western Pacific Region and KSSO criteria, obesity is defined as a body mass index (BMI) of 25 kg/m² or higher, a chronic disease that adversely affects health.
intro.line2: It should be managed as an independent disease that is a major cause of type 2 diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidemia, and cardiovascular disease.

overview.heading: What is obesity?
overview.paragraph1: Obesity is a state in which excess body fat accumulates because energy intake exceeds expenditure (WHO). In Koreans, a BMI of 25 kg/m² or higher is classified as obesity, and abdominal obesity is diagnosed when waist circumference is 90 cm or more in men and 85 cm or more in women. According to the KDCA 2022 survey, the obesity prevalence among Korean adults is about 38%, particularly high in men.
overview.paragraph2: Beyond a cosmetic issue, obesity is a key cause of metabolic syndrome, increasing the risk of type 2 diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidemia, cardio-cerebrovascular disease, fatty liver, sleep apnea, osteoarthritis, and certain cancers (colorectal, breast, etc.), and is also linked to depression.

normal.subTitle: Normal weight
normal.desc: Recommended normal BMI range for maintaining health (KSSO)

preObesity.subTitle: Pre-obesity (overweight)
preObesity.desc: Pre-obesity stage; lifestyle modification is strongly recommended

stage1.subTitle: Stage 1 obesity
stage1.desc: Stage 1 obesity; significantly increased risk of comorbidities such as diabetes and hypertension

stage2.subTitle: Stage 2 or higher obesity
stage2.desc: Stage 2-3 obesity; consider professional medical intervention, medication, and surgery

weightLoss.title: Weight loss support
weightLoss.item1: Consider prescribing GLP-1 analogs, appetite suppressants, fat absorption inhibitors, etc. for BMI 25 or higher, or BMI 23 or higher with comorbidities
weightLoss.item2: Maximize effectiveness by combining with diet and exercise therapy under a specialist's prescription

maintenance.title: Ongoing maintenance
maintenance.item1: Long-term medication use and follow-up monitoring to maintain lost weight
maintenance.item2: Continue regular counseling and lifestyle monitoring even after achieving the 5-10% initial weight loss goal

fructose.title: The dangers of high-fructose corn syrup
fructose.desc: WHO recommends limiting added sugars to less than 10% of total calories (less than 5% if possible). High-fructose corn syrup goes straight to the liver, causing fatty liver and insulin resistance.

lateNight.title: Late-night eating and metabolic disorders
lateNight.desc: Eating within 3 hours of bedtime disrupts insulin secretion rhythms, is easily stored as fat, and degrades sleep quality and the next day's balance of appetite hormones (leptin and ghrelin).

physicalActivity.title: Physical Activity Therapy (Physical Activity)
physicalActivity.desc: Per WHO recommendations, combine 150-300 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic exercise per week (brisk walking, swimming, cycling) or 75-150 minutes of high-intensity exercise with strength training at least twice a week.

cardio.title: Aerobic exercise
cardio.desc: Improves cardiorespiratory endurance and reduces body fat

strength.title: Strength training
strength.desc: Increases basal metabolic rate and prevents muscle loss

diet.title: Diet Therapy

items.0: Reduce intake by 500-750 kcal per day (target loss of 0.5-1 kg per week)
items.1: A balanced diet centered on protein and dietary fiber
items.2: Keep regular meal times and fast for 3 hours before bed

diet.note: * Starvation diets cause muscle loss and yo-yo effects, so consuming an appropriate amount of quality nutrients is key.

step1.title: Self-monitoring
step1.desc: Record meals, weight, and exercise daily to objectively understand lifestyle patterns and identify areas for improvement.

step2.title: Stress management
step2.desc: Distinguish physical hunger from emotional hunger, and prevent stress-induced binge eating with meditation and hobbies.

step3.title: Setting realistic goals
step3.desc: Set a goal of losing 5-10% of initial weight over 6 months and make a step-by-step plan to maintain a sense of achievement and motivation.

step1.title: For severe obesity with BMI 35 or higher, or BMI 30 or higher with comorbidities, metabolic and bariatric surgery such as sleeve gastrectomy may be considered.

step2.title: When comorbidities such as hypertension, diabetes, and dyslipidemia are present, establish an integrated treatment plan that addresses disease treatment and obesity management simultaneously.

ksso.source: Korean Society for the Study of Obesity (KSSO)

kdca.source: Korea Disease Control and Prevention Agency (KDCA)
kdca.detail: Korea National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey 2022, obesity prevalence

riskFactors.0: Aging and post-menopausal estrogen decline (about 38% of Korean women over 50 have osteoporosis)
riskFactors.3: Excessive drinking, smoking, and lack of physical activity

osteoporosisReportPage.title: Osteoporosis
osteoporosisReportPage.intro: By WHO definition, it is diagnosed when the DXA bone mineral density T-score is -2.5 or lower. It is a systemic skeletal disease in which fracture risk rises due to reduced bone mass and microstructural deterioration. As the population ages, the prevalence of osteoporosis among Koreans over 50 is rising rapidly, making public health management critically important.

overview.heading: What Is Osteoporosis?
overview.paragraph1: Osteoporosis is a condition in which bone strength is weakened by reduced bone mass and microstructural changes in bone tissue (WHO). Bone undergoes lifelong cycles of resorption and formation; when this balance is disrupted by menopause, aging, or certain medications, bone density decreases. According to KDCA data, about 38% of Korean women and about 7% of men over 50 have osteoporosis.
overview.paragraph2a: Osteoporosis is nearly asymptomatic as it progresses, eventually leading to
overview.paragraph2highlight: a fragile state in which spine, hip, or wrist fractures can occur from even minor trauma or everyday movements
overview.paragraph2b: . Hip fractures are a serious complication that raises the 1-year mortality rate in older adults.

diagnosis.title: Diagnostic Criteria (WHO T-score)
diagnosis.subTitle: A T-score measured from lumbar spine and femur bone density via dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA) is the standard diagnostic criterion (WHO).

normal.label: Normal
normal.desc: Bone density is stable compared to the young-adult average; maintaining preventive lifestyle habits is recommended.

osteopenia.label: Osteopenia
osteopenia.desc: A stage of reduced bone mass; calcium and vitamin D intake along with strengthened exercise are needed to prevent progression to osteoporosis.

osteoporosis.label: Osteoporosis
osteoporosis.desc: This meets the WHO diagnostic criteria and is a stage that requires active drug therapy such as bisphosphonates.

antiresorptive.heading: Anti-resorptive Agents
antiresorptive.item2: Denosumab - an injection given once every 6 months

anabolic.heading: Anabolic (Bone-forming) Agents
anabolic.item2: Romosozumab - a powerful agent introduced recently

calcium.title: Calcium Intake
calcium.desc: 800-1,000 mg per day recommended for those over 50 (milk, anchovies, tofu, kale)

vitaminD.title: Vitamin D
vitaminD.desc: 800-1,000 IU per day recommended, combined with sun exposure 2-3 times a week

weightBearing.title: Weight-Bearing Exercise
weightBearing.desc: Walking, jogging, or stair climbing 3-5 times a week, 150+ minutes per week as recommended by WHO

fallPrevention.heading: Fall Prevention
fallPrevention.intro: Most fractures in older adults are caused by falls, so creating an environment that prevents falls is as important as strengthening bones (Korean Society for Bone and Mineral Research, KSBMR).
fallPrevention.check1: Ensure sufficient indoor lighting and remove floor obstacles
fallPrevention.check2: Install non-slip mats and handrails in bathrooms and on stairs
fallPrevention.check3: Regular vision and hearing checks and balance-improving exercises (e.g., tai chi)

fractureRisk.heading: Fracture Risk
fractureRisk.desc: Fractures of the wrist, spine, and hip are most common; hip fractures in particular are so serious that the 1-year mortality rate reaches about 15-20%. The spine can suffer a compression fracture from even minor trauma or a cough.

bodyChange.title: Body Shape Changes
bodyChange.desc: As spinal compression fractures accumulate, you may lose 3 cm or more in height or develop kyphosis (a hunched back).

dullPain.title: Dull Pain
dullPain.desc: Osteoporosis itself is asymptomatic, but when microfractures or compression fractures occur, chronic lower-back pain and generalized pain may accompany them.

ksbmr.source: Korean Society for Bone and Mineral Research (KSBMR)

kdca.source: Korea Disease Control and Prevention Agency (KDCA)
kdca.detail: Korea National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey 2022, Osteoporosis Prevalence

empty.label: Before entering values
empty.comment: Enter systolic and diastolic values to check your current blood pressure status.
empty.tip: Entering right after measuring makes record management easier.

low.label: Low blood pressure
low.comment: Your blood pressure appears to be on the low side.
low.tip: If you feel dizzy or fatigued, get plenty of rest and check your condition.

high.label: High blood pressure
high.comment: Your blood pressure appears to be on the high side.
high.tip: If it's repeatedly high, review your lifestyle and consider consulting a specialist if needed.

caution.label: Caution range
caution.comment: It's measured slightly above the normal range.
caution.tip: It helps to check factors like salty food, caffeine, and stress.

normal.label: Normal range
normal.comment: Your blood pressure trend looks stable.
normal.tip: Recording consistently like this makes it easier to track changes.

info.normal: Normal blood pressure: systolic below 120mmHg, diastolic below 80mmHg
info.high: Hypertension: systolic 140mmHg or higher, or diastolic 90mmHg or higher
info.low: Hypotension: generally systolic 90mmHg or below, or diastolic 60mmHg or below
